summary: O'Neil reviews Bethany McLean's 'Shaky Ground,' which traces the bizarre history of Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac from the Great Depression through the 2008 crisis to present day. She highlights how these mortgage giants were placed in conservatorship rather than nationalized primarily for accounting optics, and how their recent profits have been entirely siphoned to the Treasury despite still having shareholders. The post catalogs the massive government subsidies for homeownership while noting that homeownership rates are actually declining, especially for minorities. O'Neil concludes that the entire system is irrational, politically entangled, and that strengthening renter protections makes more sense than continuing to prop up homeownership mythology.
key_points: Fannie and Freddie were kept in conservatorship rather than nationalized primarily because nationalization would have added their debts to the national deficit
Hedge funds who bought shares cheaply are suing because all profits now go to Treasury, leaving shareholders with nothing
The 'American dream' justification for these entities is undermined by the fact that most mid-2000s loans were for refinancing and second homes, not first-time homebuyers
Government support through Fannie/Freddie plus mortgage tax deductions dramatically inflates housing prices while homeownership rates, especially for minorities, continue to decline
The current system leaves no financial cushion for another crisis, yet there's no political will to change anything
money_quotes: Speaking as a non-homeowner, I think this is crap.
People still 'hate' Fannie and Freddie but nobody seems to have a plan to transition to a system whereby the government isn't the final backstop for mortgages.

The combination of the cheap backstop represented by Fannie and Freddie, in addition to the mortgage tax deduction represents a FUCKTON of government support for homeownership, and also raises the prices of houses by an extreme amount.
It's fascinating stuff, and also completely baffling as well, partly because it's so deeply entwined with politics, so there's really not a lot of rational logic attached to it.
After reading this book, I can only conclude that it's time to strengthen renter laws.
Most of the loans in the mid-2000's were for refinancing and for second homes, so it wasn't like that was actually happening.
key_insight: The U.S. mortgage system funnels enormous government resources into inflating housing prices under the guise of promoting homeownership, while actual ownership rates decline and renters get nothing.
